perturbation.original_text: The Blockchain Administrator shall also, on behalf of the Fund, conduct relations with custodians, depositories, transfer agents, dividend disbursing agents, other stockholder servicing agents, accountants, attorneys, underwriters, brokers and dealers, corporate fiduciaries, insurers, banks and such other persons as the Blockchain Administrator shall deem to be necessary or desirable in connection with the issuance and transfer of the Fund's ERC-1404 compatible digital security,.
perturbation.changed_text: The Blockchain Administrator shall also, on behalf of the Fund, consider conducting relations with custodians, depositories, transfer agents, dividend disbursing agents, other stockholder servicing agents, accountants, attorneys, underwriters, brokers and dealers, corporate fiduciaries, insurers, banks and such other persons as the Blockchain Administrator shall occasionally deem appropriate in connection with the issuance and transfer of the Fund's ERC-1404 compatible digital security,.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'conduct relations' to 'consider conducting relations' and 'shall deem to be necessary or desirable' to 'shall occasionally deem appropriate' introduces ambiguity and discretion, weakening the obligation to maintain necessary relationships for fund operation. The term 'occasionally' further reduces the stringency of the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 31(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires registered investment companies to maintain accurate records and accounts. By making the relationships with essential service providers discretionary ('consider conducting relations', 'occasionally deem appropriate'), the modified text creates a loophole where the Blockchain Administrator might neglect these relationships, potentially leading to inadequate record-keeping and compliance with the Act's requirements. This contradicts the intention of maintaining robust oversight and accurate accounting practices as required by the Act.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-30 - Accounts and records | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-30 - Accounts and records U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Maintenance of records (1) In general Each registered investment company , and each underwriter, broker, dealer, or investment adviser that is a majority-owned subsidiary of such a company, shall maintain and preserve such records (as defined in section 78c(a)(37) of this title ) for such period or periods as the Commission, by rules and regulations, may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. Each investment adviser that is not a majority-owned subsidiary of, and each depositor of any registered investment company, and each principal underwriter for any registered investment company other than a closed-end company, shall maintain and preserve for such period or periods as the Commission shall prescribe by rules and regulations, such records as are necessary or appropriate to record such person’ s transactions with such registered company. Each person having custody or use of the securities, deposits, or credits of a registered investment company shall maintain and preserve all records that relate to the custody or use by such person of the securities, deposits, or credits of the registered investment company for such period or periods as the Commission, by rule or regulation, may prescribe, as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (2) Minimizing compliance burden In exercising its authority under this subsection, the Commission shall take such steps as it deems necessary or appropriate, consistent with the public interest and for the protection of investors, to avoid unnecessary recordkeeping by, and minimize the compliance burden on, persons required to maintain records under this
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'conduct relations' with 'consider conducting relations' and 'shall deem to be necessary or desirable' with 'shall occasionally deem appropriate,' weakening the obligation to maintain essential relationships. The Law Explanation highlights that Section 31(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires registered investment companies to maintain accurate records and accounts. Scraped Law Snippet 2, derived from 15 U.S. Code § 80a-30, reinforces this by stating that registered investment companies 'shall maintain and preserve such records' as the Commission prescribes. The shift to discretionary relationships ('consider' and 'occasionally') directly contradicts the requirement to 'maintain and preserve' records, as it introduces the possibility of neglecting necessary relationships for compliance, leading to non-compliance with the Act.

perturbation.original_text: The Blockchain Administrator agrees to maintain and keep all books, accounts and other records of the Fund that relate to activities performed by the Blockchain Administrator hereunder and will maintain and keep such books, accounts and records in accordance with the Investment Company Act.
perturbation.changed_text: The Blockchain Administrator will endeavor to maintain books, accounts, and records of the Fund related to its activities hereunder and will attempt to maintain such books, accounts, and records in general accordance with principles of the Investment Company Act.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to maintain and keep' to 'will endeavor to maintain' and 'in accordance with the Investment Company Act' to 'in general accordance with principles of the Investment Company Act' introduces ambiguity, weakening the obligation to accurately maintain records as required by the Investment Company Act. Replacing 'agrees' with 'endeavor' implies that maintaining records is not a guaranteed obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 31a-1 under the Investment Company Act of 1940 mandates specific record-keeping requirements for registered investment companies. The modified text weakens the obligation by using terms like 'endeavor to maintain' and 'in general accordance with principles,' making compliance with specific requirements discretionary rather than mandatory. This could lead to inadequate or incomplete records, which violates the rule's intention for detailed and accurate record-keeping.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation to maintain accurate records according to the Investment Company Act. By changing 'agrees to maintain and keep' to 'will endeavor to maintain' and 'in accordance with the Investment Company Act' to 'in general accordance with principles of the Investment Company Act,' the modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the mandatory nature of record-keeping. The Law Explanation supports that Rule 31a-1 mandates specific record-keeping, and the modified text makes compliance discretionary, potentially leading to inadequate records. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms that registered investment companies must maintain records.

perturbation.original_text: The Blockchain Administrator shall ensure that any such service provider shall operate in conformity with the requirements of the Investment Company Act and other applicable federal and state law and shall maintain books and records of the Fund (if any) in a manner substantially similar to Section 2 of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Blockchain Administrator will generally encourage any such service provider to operate with consideration for the requirements of the Investment Company Act and other potentially applicable federal and state law and may suggest they maintain books and records of the Fund (if any) in a manner similar to Section 2 of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall ensure' to 'will generally encourage', 'operate in conformity with' to 'operate with consideration for', 'applicable federal and state law' to 'potentially applicable federal and state law', and 'shall maintain' to 'may suggest they maintain' significantly weakens the oversight responsibility of the Blockchain Administrator, potentially resulting in non-compliance by service providers. The terms 'generally encourage,' 'consideration for,' and 'potentially applicable' introduce significant ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17(e)(1) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 restricts affiliated persons from acting as agent and accepting compensation for the purchase or sale of property to or for the investment company. By weakening the requirement for service providers to comply with the Act, the Blockchain Administrator may fail to prevent affiliated persons from engaging in prohibited transactions, such as accepting compensation from third parties that could be detrimental to the Fund. The modified text introduces ambiguity that makes it difficult to enforce proper conduct by service providers, undermining the protections against conflicts of interest provided by the Act.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-17 - Transactions of certain affiliated persons and underwriters | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-17 - Transactions of certain affiliated persons and underwriters U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Prohibited transactions It shall be unlawful for any affiliated person or promoter of or principal underwriter for a registered investment company (other than a company of the character described in section 80a–12(d)(3)(A) and (B) of this title), or any affiliated person of such a person, promoter, or principal underwriter, acting as principal— (1) knowingly to sell any security or other property to such registered company or to any company controlled by such registered company , unless such sale involves solely (A) securities of which the buyer is the issuer, (B) securities of which the seller is the issuer and which are part of a general offering to the holders of a class of its securities, or (C) securities deposited with the trustee of a unit investment trust or periodic payment plan by the depositor thereof; (2) knowingly to purchase from such registered company , or from any company controlled by such registered company , any security or other property (except securities of which the seller is the issuer) ; (3) to borrow money or other property from such registered company or from any company controlled by such registered company (unless the borrower is controlled by the lender) except as permitted in section 80a–21(b) of this title ; or (4) to loan money or other property to such registered company , or to any company controlled by such registered company , in contravention of such rules, regulations, or orders as the Commission may, after consultation with and taking into consideration the views of the Federal banking agencies (as defined in section 1813 of title 12 ), prescribe or issue consistent w
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities that weaken the Blockchain Administrator's responsibility to ensure service provider compliance with the Investment Company Act. The original text's 'shall ensure' and 'operate in conformity with' create a clear obligation, while the revised text's 'will generally encourage' and 'operate with consideration for' significantly reduce this obligation. The law snippet from 15 U.S. Code § 80a-17 addresses transactions of certain affiliated persons and underwriters, stating that 'It shall be unlawful for any affiliated person...acting as principal' to engage in prohibited transactions. By weakening the compliance requirement, the modified text increases the risk of affiliated persons engaging in unlawful transactions detrimental to the Fund, directly contradicting the intent of the law to prevent such activities. The changes introduce enough ambiguity that the Blockchain Administrator could argue they met the contract's obligations while still failing to prevent illegal actions.
